title: A-Mem: Agentic Memory for LLM Agents
summary: This paper presents A-MEM, a memory system for LLM agents that dynamically organizes memories without relying on predefined structures. Inspired by the Zettelkasten method, it creates an interconnected knowledge network through atomic notes, link generation, and memory evolution. When a new memory is added, the system generates a structured note with keywords, tags, and a contextual description. It then retrieves the top-k most similar historical memories using cosine similarity on embeddings, and uses an LLM to decide whether to create links between the new and existing memories. The new memory can also trigger updates to the contextual descriptions and tags of existing memories (memory evolution). At retrieval time, a query is embedded and the most relevant memories are returned, along with linked neighbors. Experiments across six foundation models on long-term conversational QA datasets show superior performance over LoCoMo, ReadAgent, MemoryBank, and MemGPT, with significant reductions in token usage and retrieval latency.
key_insights: **Atomic note structure with LLM-generated attributes**: For each interaction, store a note containing: original content, timestamp, LLM-generated keywords, LLM-generated tags, an LLM-generated contextual description, and an embedding vector computed from all textual components. This multi-faceted representation enables both semantic similarity search and nuanced LLM-based reasoning for linking and evolution.

**Two-phase memory linking**: First, use dense retrieval (cosine similarity on embeddings) to find the top-k most relevant historical memories. Second, feed the new note and those candidate memories to an LLM to decide which connections to establish. This combines efficient filtering with deep semantic understanding, avoiding rigid predefined schemas.

**Memory evolution as a continuous update policy**: When a new memory is added, not only is it linked to existing memories, but the nearest neighbors themselves may be updated—their context descriptions, keywords, and tags can be revised by the LLM to reflect insights from the new memory. This allows the memory network to refine its understanding over time, analogous to human learning.

**Retrieval that returns linked neighborhoods**: At query time, compute the query embedding, retrieve the top-k most similar memory notes, and also automatically return memories linked to those top-k via the same 'box' (group of interconnected memories). This enriches the context presented to the agent without increasing retrieval cost proportionally.

**Agentic, not static**: The system replaces fixed memory operations (predefined schemas, fixed write/retrieve points in the agent workflow) with LLM-driven decisions about when and how to link, tag, and update. This enables the memory to adapt to diverse tasks and evolving knowledge without manual reconfiguration.

**Practical scalability**: The system shows O(N) storage scaling and near-constant retrieval time (0.31 µs to 3.70 µs for 1K to 1M memories) using standard dense embedding search. Token cost per memory operation is low (~1,200 tokens), making deployment economically viable.

**Ablation validates both linking and evolution**: Removing both link generation and memory evolution causes severe performance drops, especially on multi-hop tasks. Removing only evolution still degrades performance, showing that both components contribute complementary benefits.
